title: Instruction Lens Score: Your Instruction Contributes a Powerful Object Hallucination Detector for Multimodal Large Language Models
abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress, yet the object hallucination remains a critical challenge for reliable deployment. In this paper, we present an in-depth analysis of instruction token embeddings and reveal that they implicitly encode visual information while effectively filtering erroneous information introduced by misleading visual embeddings.  Building on this insight, we propose the Instruction Lens Score (InsLen), which combines a Calibrated Local Score with a Context Consistency Score that measures context consistency of the object tokens.  The proposed approach serves as a plug-and-play object hallucination detector without relying on auxiliary models or additional training.  Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and diverse MLLM architectures demonstrate that InsLen consistently outperforms existing hallucination detection methods, highlighting its effectiveness and robustness. The code will be publicly available.
decision_comment:
The main tension comes from reviewer V8kB for requesting of the theoretical analysis. Frankly speaking, I think the reviewer should be more specific about what kind of theoretical analysis is required. Based on my understanding the reviewer might require a logic reasoning why a generic instruction such as “Please describe the image in detail.” may reflect language priors more strongly than actual visual evidence. The reviewer might need a more concrete reasoning argument (not empirical evidences) However the author might understand this as finding a theoretical background for the proposed approach. 

Frankly speaking, I think this reviewer comment is not very well constructed and the answer might not fully address the reviewer's comment. However I don't think the author should be punished based on this. And all the other three reviewers vote for weak accept. I agree with them and vote for weak accept.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Instruction Lens Score (InsLen), a training-free, plug-and-play detector for object hallucinations (OH) in multimodal LLMs. The key insight is that instruction token embeddings, when examined via the Logit Lens, implicitly encode image-grounded visual semantics and tend to downweight misleading signals from image embeddings. InsLen combines (i) a Calibrated Local Score that modulates vision-based local evidence with an instruction-derived calibration confidence, and (ii) a Context Consistency Score that measures the consistency between generated object-token embeddings and an aggregate of instruction embeddings most supportive of that object. Across five MLLMs and multiple benchmarks (MSCOCO, Objects365, POPE, CLEVR), InsLen outperforms state-of-the-art detection methods.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths  
1. Leveraging instruction token embeddings for OH detection is a fresh angle compared to prior methods that focus on visual tokens, attention, or global-local image-text similarities.  
2. Results span diverse MLLMs (LLaVA-1.5, InstructBLIP, mPLUG-Owl3, LLaVA-OneVision, Qwen3-VL) and multiple settings (caption-style OH, yes/no POPE, attribute/relational CLEVR), demonstrating robustness and generality.  
3. Ablations disentangle contributions of the calibration factor and the context-consistency weighting, and sensitivity analyses examine the trade-off parameter ω and number m of instruction embeddings.  
### Weakness  
1. The reliance on unembedding projections (Logit Lens) to interpret instruction embeddings assumes the language head is a faithful probe of cross-modal content; this interpretability proxy can be noisy or layer-dependent.  
2. The context consistency uses an L2-based score with a hand-tuned α, raising concerns about scale sensitivity and cross-model comparability; cosine or learned Mahalanobis variants are not explored.  
3. Potential vulnerability to instruction manipulation: prompts that implicitly or explicitly name objects could inflate S_cafe/S_ccs and weaken hallucination detection.  
4. Limited analysis of layer choice and temperature τ; results may depend on the penultimate layer and τ=10, but these sensitivities are not reported.  
5. Runtime/compute overhead versus baselines (especially for projecting all instruction tokens and computing Top-K image embeddings per object) is not quantified.  
6. POPE adaptation details are under-specified (e.g., how token-level scoring maps to yes/no answers and whether negative questions bias S_cafe).

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes Instruction Lens Score (InsLen), a training-free method for detecting object hallucinations in multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The authors analyze instruction token embeddings and observe that they implicitly encode visual semantics and can suppress misleading signals from image embeddings. The authors strive to explore a central context in which instruction embeddings act as a reliable signal for identifying hallucinated objects during multimodal generation.

Based on this observation, the authors outline an important concept: instruction embeddings can be used to calibrate visual evidence and provide contextual cues for hallucination detection. The proposed InsLen score combines two components: (1) Calibrated Local Score, which adjusts vision-based similarity scores using confidence derived from instruction embeddings, and (2) Context Consistency Score, which measures the consistency between generated object token embeddings and aggregated instruction embeddings associated with that object.

The final score integrates these two signals to determine whether a generated object token corresponds to a real object in the image. The method is plug-and-play and does not require additional training or external models. Experiments on multiple benchmarks and several MLLMs show that InsLen consistently outperforms existing hallucination detection approaches.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

Novel insight. The paper identifies an interesting and underexplored signal in MLLMs: instruction embeddings can implicitly encode grounded visual information and help suppress misleading visual cues.

Training-free and plug-and-play. The proposed method does not require additional training, external models, or extra supervision, making it easy to integrate with existing MLLMs.

Strong empirical results. Experiments across multiple datasets  and different MLLM architectures demonstrate consistent improvements over several recent baselines.


### Weaknesses

Dependence on prompt structure. Since the method relies on instruction embeddings, performance may vary depending on the instruction wording or prompt length, which raises concerns about robustness in diverse prompting scenarios.

Focus on object hallucination only. The method is mainly designed for object-level hallucinations, and its effectiveness for other hallucination types (e.g., attributes, relationships, reasoning errors, OCR) is less explored.

Limited qualitative analysis. The paper would benefit from more detailed case studies illustrating when and why instruction embeddings succeed or fail in detecting hallucinations.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Sensitivity to instruction prompts.
Since the proposed method relies on instruction embeddings, how sensitive is InsLen to different prompt formulations or instruction styles? Would the performance degrade when using shorter, paraphrased, or task-specific prompts?

Generalization beyond object hallucination.
The current method mainly targets object-level hallucinations. Can the proposed approach be extended to detect other types of hallucinations, such as OCR?

Computational overhead.
The method requires extracting intermediate embeddings and computing additional similarity scores. What is the inference-time overhead compared to standard generation?

Failure cases.
Could the authors provide more qualitative analysis or failure cases where instruction embeddings fail to detect hallucinations, particularly when hallucinated objects are visually similar to real ones?
reviews.limitations: The paper briefly discusses some limitations, but the discussion could be further strengthened. In particular, the method mainly focuses on object-level hallucination detection, and its applicability to other types of multimodal hallucinations (e.g., attribute errors, relational mistakes, or OCR-related textual hallucinations) is not explored. In addition, the reliance on instruction embeddings may introduce sensitivity to prompt design, which could affect robustness in real-world deployments. A more explicit discussion of these limitations and potential impacts would improve the paper.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes the Instruction Lens Score (InsLen), a plug-and-play object hallucination detector for multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The key insight is that instruction token embeddings implicitly encode visual information while filtering out misleading visual signals. InsLen combines a Calibrated Local Score (which calibrates vision-based confidence using instruction embeddings) with a Context Consistency Score (which measures agreement between generated object tokens and object-related instruction embeddings). The method requires no auxiliary models or additional training and is evaluated across multiple MLLM architectures and hallucination benchmarks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
## Strengths
- The observation that instruction embeddings filter misleading visual information is genuinely interesting and well-supported by the Logit Lens analysis (Figure 1, Figure 2). This is a previously overlooked signal for hallucination detection.
- The method requires no auxiliary models, external data, or fine-tuning, making it highly practical for deployment across different MLLM architectures.
- Experiments span multiple MLLMs (LLaVA-1.5, LLaVA-OneVision1.5, Qwen3-VL, mPLUG-Owl3) and benchmarks (MSCOCO, etc.), showing consistent improvements on AUROC over prior methods.
- The two-component score (calibrated local + context consistency) is logically motivated — one addresses local visual evidence calibration, the other captures global contextual coherence.
- The paper is well-organized with informative figures illustrating the core insight.

## Weaknesses
- The paper does not cite or discuss Visual Contrastive Decoding (VCD, Leng et al., 2023), a prominent training-free approach to hallucination mitigation that also leverages contrastive signals from visual inputs. While VCD targets hallucination *mitigation* during generation rather than post-hoc *detection*, the two approaches are closely related in motivation and technique.
- On LLaVA-1.5 and InstructBLIP (Table 3), AUROC values with some baselines below random. The paper acknowledges "limited answer accuracy" but does not sufficiently discuss when and why the method fails.
- The method requires choosing a specific decoder layer per model with no discussion of how these were selected or how sensitive results are to this choice. This is a critical practical deployment concern.
- The paper does not adequately discuss when InsLen fails.
- The method relies on instruction token embeddings, but different prompt templates and instruction styles could significantly affect the filtering behavior. The paper lacks ablation over different instruction formats.
- Computing confidence across all instruction embeddings for every object token could be expensive for long instructions or many generated objects. Computational overhead analysis is insufficient.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. How sensitive is InsLen to the specific instruction template used? Have you tested with significantly different instruction formats (e.g., single-word prompts vs. detailed multi-sentence instructions)?
2. What is the computational overhead of InsLen relative to the base MLLM inference? How does it scale with instruction length and number of generated objects?
3. Can you provide a failure case analysis? What types of hallucinations does InsLen consistently miss, and why?
reviews.limitations: No. The paper has no Limitations section and no Impact Statement. The conclusion (Section 6) is purely positive with no acknowledgment of the method's scope or failure modes.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new method, called Instruction Lens Score (InsLen), to detect object hallucinations in multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The authors observe that instruction embeddings may implicitly filter misleading visual information introduced by image embeddings and leverage this property to design a hallucination detection mechanism. Specifically, the proposed InsLen score combines a Calibrated Local Score and a Context Consistency Score to jointly utilize local visual evidence and global contextual information. Through experiments on multiple benchmarks, including MSCOCO, Objects365, POPE, and CLEVR, and across several MLLM architectures, the authors demonstrate that the proposed method achieves improved performance compared to existing hallucination detection approaches.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Strengths**

1.The paper presents an interesting perspective by analyzing the role of instruction embeddings in multimodal large language models and exploring how they can be utilized for hallucination detection. In particular, the observation that instruction embeddings may suppress misleading signals originating from visual embeddings provides a useful insight into the internal behavior of MLLMs. This analysis could potentially inspire further research on understanding and interpreting multimodal representations.

2.The proposed method is training-free and plug-and-play, meaning that it can be applied without additional model training or external auxiliary models. Compared to prior approaches that rely on large external models such as GPT-4 for hallucination detection, this design makes the method computationally lighter and easier to integrate into existing MLLM systems.

3.The paper also presents relatively comprehensive experimental evaluations across multiple models and benchmarks. Experiments are conducted on several MLLM architectures, including LLaVA, InstructBLIP, Qwen-VL, and mPLUG-Owl, as well as multiple evaluation datasets such as MSCOCO, Objects365, POPE, and CLEVR. In addition, the authors include ablation studies and sensitivity analyses that provide further insights into the contributions of the individual components of the proposed method.

**Weaknesses**

1.The primary concern lies in the core assumption that instruction embeddings can effectively filter misleading visual information. Although this claim is empirically supported throughout the paper, it does not appear to be grounded in a clear theoretical justification. For instance, a generic instruction such as “Please describe the image in detail.” may reflect language priors more strongly than actual visual evidence. As a result, it is difficult to determine whether the observed performance improvement truly arises from improved visual grounding or from prompt-induced linguistic bias. To better support the main claim, stronger theoretical justification in addition to empirical evidence would be necessary.

2.Another concern is the reliance on heuristically designed scoring components. The InsLen score combines several heuristic elements, including calibration confidence, context consistency score, and the weighting factor 𝜔. Similar to the first issue, many aspects of this design appear to be empirically motivated without clear theoretical grounding. Moreover, since the method depends on multiple hyperparameters, the performance may vary depending on their settings. Although some sensitivity analyses are provided, it remains unclear whether the approach can generalize robustly across different settings without careful tuning.

3.In addition, recent studies (e.g., “Temporal Reasoning Transfer from Text to Video,” ICLR 2025) suggest that hallucination or reasoning issues in MLLMs may originate from the language model itself rather than from image embeddings. Therefore, approaches that attempt to improve MLLM performance by leveraging instruction or text embeddings are not entirely new. It would be beneficial for the paper to more clearly articulate how the proposed method overcomes the limitations of prior work and what aspects of the approach are truly novel compared to existing research.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Please refer to the Weaknesses section for detailed concerns. 

In particular, beyond the empirical observations presented in the paper, it seems crucial to provide a clearer theoretical justification or background explaining why instruction embeddings can effectively filter misleading visual information or be related to the cause of object hallucination. Strengthening this aspect would help clarify the core assumption and improve the overall persuasiveness of the paper.

In addition, the proposed method consists of several heuristic components and multiple processing steps. It may be worth considering whether a simpler and more principled approach with fewer hyperparameters could achieve similar or better performance. If possible, the authors are encouraged to discuss potential directions for simplifying and refining the method to make it more concise and robust.
reviews.limitations: No. See the Weaknesses section and Key Questions for Authors section.
